metadata.title: The Building That Heats Empty Rooms for Forty Years

beats.narration_text: Hei — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'Why Your Office Building Burns $50,000 on Empty Rooms' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. Why do office buildings waste so much energy? Not a technology problem. An organizational one. The static schedules programmed in 1985 were never revisited — because revisiting them required a ladder and a technician and was never prioritized. Forty percent of HVAC savings in the study came from turning off systems running in zones confirmed empty. Nobody had checked.

props.segment: The Building That Heats Empty Rooms for Forty Years
props.command: Why do office buildings waste so much energy?
props.runningText: reading smart building HVAC optimization case study…
output: 40% of HVAC savings: turning off systems in zones confirmed empty — nobody had checked.
1985 BAS schedule: required a ladder and a technician to update. Never was.
15-building portfolio: 25% HVAC energy savings in one year.

beats.act: building heatmap — empty zones with cost
beats.narration_text: Scan the building zone by zone. Floor one, east wing: occupancy sensors confirm empty from 6 PM. HVAC running at full capacity. Cost per hour: visible. Floor two, conference rooms: empty all day Monday, HVAC running. Floor three, open office: half occupied but HVAC serving as if full. Zone by zone, the building reveals itself: the static 1985 schedule assumed Monday-through-Friday, 8 AM to 6 PM, full occupancy. The reality is patchy occupancy, irregular hours, and a schedule nobody updated because the building automation system required physical access and a specialist to change it. The waste was not invisible — it was just never looked at.

manim.description: Building floor plan. Zones scan sequentially. Empty zones light up in red with cost-per-hour annotation. Occupied zones in white. Pattern reveals most red zones correspond to off-schedule hours or underutilized areas. 1985 schedule boundary drawn as horizontal bar. Gap between schedule and reality shaded.

beats.narration_text: Three categories of savings from the 15-building portfolio study. After-hours waste: systems running when occupancy sensors confirmed zero occupancy. This is the largest category — 40% of the savings. Fault detection: sensors detected HVAC units running continuously without cycling, indicating stuck dampers, failed actuators, and other equipment faults that the static schedule masked. Setpoint drift: thermostats configured to 68 degrees that had drifted to 72 degrees over years — four degrees of unnecessary heating or cooling, invisible without a monitoring system. Together, the three categories produced 25% HVAC energy savings in one year across 15 buildings.

animated_deck.description: Stacked bar chart with three savings categories. After-hours waste (largest, 40% of savings), Fault detection (medium), Setpoint drift (smaller). Total bar: 25% HVAC savings. Each category color-coded. Labels show mechanism of each waste type.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Forty percent of HVAC savings came from turning off systems running in zones confirmed empty — nobody had checked. The 1985 building automation schedule required a ladder and a technician to update; it never was. A 15-building portfolio achieved 25% HVAC energy savings in one year from AI-powered IoT monitoring. The waste was not technical — it was organizational. The building knew exactly what it was doing. Nobody had asked.

props.artifactTitle: The Building That Heats Empty Rooms for Forty Years
props.artifactHeading: An organizational problem, not a technical one
artifactLines: 40% of HVAC savings: empty-zone shutdown — nobody had checked.
1985 BAS schedule: required specialist to update, never was updated.
25% HVAC savings in one year across a 15-building portfolio.
The waste was organizational. The building knew. Nobody asked.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Audit a building's HVAC schedule against its actual occupancy data. At what hours does the schedule deviate most from reality? What would a cost-optimal schedule look like?' The audit structure is simple: plot actual occupancy from sensors or badge data against the scheduled HVAC runtime. The gap between those two curves, multiplied by the energy cost per hour, is the waste. Find the largest gap. That is where the optimization starts. Liam, in for Bear.
